serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of coconut cream used in cooking is approximately 2 tablespoons (30 g), providing around 220 kcal and 20.7 g of fat.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut cream — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for coconut cream (first pressing). Saturated fat value cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 12118 (Coconut cream, raw) which reports 59.7 g saturated fat per 100 g; HPB-derived total fat of 69.0 g is consistent with first-press concentration. Saturated fat estimated at 61.0 g per 100 g based on USDA SR Legacy proportional ratio (86.5% of total fat as saturated fat) applied to HPB total fat figure.

gi_source.title: Coconut cream — analogous estimate based on coconut-derived products; University of Sydney GI Database consulted
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for coconut cream is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Coconut cream contains 27.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g, but the dominant fat matrix (69 g/100 g, predominantly saturated) substantially blunts any glycaemic response. An analogous estimate of GI 45 (low category) is applied, consistent with high-fat coconut-derived foods where fat markedly slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption. Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as: (45 × 27.9 × 0.30) / 100 = approximately 3.4 g. This is a conservative analogous estimate; actual GI may be lower given the extreme fat content.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high saturated fat content (~61 g/100 g) contributing to arterial stiffness and cardiovascular risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit coconut cream to trace flavouring quantities only, as its saturated fat content far exceeds the 5 g/100 g threshold and contributes to arterial stiffness and elevated cardiovascular risk; substitution with low-fat coconut milk or evaporated skim milk is recommended.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high caloric density (733 kcal/100 g) and saturated fat content impairing insulin sensitivity and promoting weight gain
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should use coconut cream only sparingly as a flavouring agent, as its extremely high fat content (69 g/100 g, predominantly saturated) impairs insulin sensitivity, promotes weight gain, and worsens glycaemic control; it should not be used as a primary cooking medium.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at ~61 g/100 g raises LDL cholesterol and substantially increases cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of coconut cream, as its saturated fat content of approximately 61 g/100 g substantially raises LDL cholesterol and increases atherosclerotic risk; use should be restricted to occasional trace amounts for flavouring only.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at ~61 g/100 g strongly promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory cytokines relevant to RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid coconut cream as its saturated fat content (~61 g/100 g) massively exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold and strongly promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; replacement with anti-inflammatory oils such as extra-virgin olive oil is strongly recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coconut cream is the thick, rich extract obtained from the first pressing of freshly grated mature coconut flesh. Unlike coconut milk, which is produced by diluting the grated coconut with water before pressing, coconut cream is the undiluted first-press product and therefore has a much higher fat concentration. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 733 kcal, 69 g of total fat (of which approximately 61 g is saturated fat, predominantly lauric acid and myristic acid), 27.9 g of carbohydrate, 0.97 g of sugar, 0.8 g of dietary fibre, 0.2 g of protein, and 33 mg of sodium. The texture is dense, creamy, and almost paste-like at room temperature, with a rich, sweet coconut aroma and flavour. It is a foundational ingredient in Southeast Asian cuisines, particularly in Singaporean, Malaysian, Indonesian, and Thai cooking, where it is used as the base for curries, laksa, rendang, desserts such as kueh and bubur cha cha, and rice dishes such as nasi lemak. Clinically, coconut cream is one of the most calorie-dense and saturated-fat-rich foods in common culinary use. Its saturated fat content far exceeds recommended thresholds for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. It should be used only in small quantities as a flavouring agent rather than as a primary cooking medium for individuals with these conditions. Despite being plant-based and dairy-free, it is not suitable for low-fat or heart-healthy dietary patterns.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 12118 using proportional ratio method, clearly documented. Food identity is unambiguous. Source is a primary approved regional database.
notes: Saturated fat (61.0 g/100 g) is estimated by applying the USDA SR Legacy saturated-fat-to-total-fat ratio (approximately 86.5%) to the HPB total fat value of 69.0 g/100 g; this is documented as an adapted value. Monounsaturated fat (3.0 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.8 g/100 g) are similarly estimated from USDA SR Legacy proportional ratios. Trans fat is set to 0.0 g as coconut cream is a plant-derived product with no industrial hydrogenation; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg as confirmed by HPB source; coconut cream is a plant-based product and contains no dietary cholesterol — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. The following fields are set to -1 (data not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA SR Legacy as of 2026-04-16): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Glycemic index is set to 45 (low category) as an analogous estimate: no direct GI value for coconut cream is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database; the estimate reflects the substantial fat-mediated blunting of glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 30 g serving is approximately 3.4 g. Protein_g is 0.2 g — not zero; no zero-macro justification required for protein. Fat_g is 69.0 g — not zero. Carbohydrate_g is 27.9 g — not zero.
